evidence.note: confirm-verdict sends socket op "confirm" via sendVerdictOverrideRequest.
veto-verdict sends socket op "veto_verdict" via sendVerdictOverrideRequest.
buildSocketRouter registers exactly 25 ops; none is "confirm", "veto_verdict", "confirm_verdict", or any verdict-override op.
Unregistered ops fall through to the default: SocketResponse{Ok:false, Error:'daemon: unknown op %q'}.
The only daemon pre-branches are op=="subscribe" and hook-relay; neither handles confirm/veto.
The core VerdictOverride domain logic exists but is never invoked from any daemon dispatch/handler path (only referenced by tests) — it is not wired to the socket router.

reasoning: Both CLI commands open the daemon unix socket and send a verdict-override request. confirm-verdict sends op "confirm"; veto-verdict sends op "veto_verdict". buildSocketRouter in socketdispatch.go registers 25 ops and none of them is any verdict-override op. A whole-tree grep for the op-name strings finds no router.Register, no case, and no alternate spelling that handles them; the subscribe/hook-relay pre-branches in socket.go do not either. Any unregistered op falls to the default 'daemon: unknown op' response. Note an additional latent defect: confirm-verdict sends "confirm" while the shared helper's doc claims the op should be "confirm_verdict" — but this is moot since neither spelling is registered. The core VerdictOverride logic (verdictoverride_rc027.go) is implemented but never reachable from the daemon dispatch path. Therefore a run blocked on a confirm_required reconciliation verdict has no working socket-side release path via these commands.
